planting.note: Start indoors 8–10 weeks before last frost; aji limon is a Peruvian species (Capsicum baccatum) that germinates more slowly than C. annuum peppers and benefits from a longer indoor growing period. Needs consistently warm conditions to thrive.

directions: Start indoors in warm conditions and transplant after frost danger has passed; lemon drop pepper (aji limon) is a Peruvian Capsicum baccatum cultivar producing small, tapered, bright yellow fruits 2–3 inches long with a distinctive intensely fruity, citrusy, lemon-like flavor combined with a clean, building heat (30,000–50,000 SHU). The lemon aroma and flavor make it quite different from other yellow hot peppers and it is used extensively in Peruvian cuisine — in ceviche, aji de gallina, and fresh salsas where its fruitiness and brightness are essential. The plants are productive and quite ornamental with their pendant yellow fruits. Fresh or dried and ground, it is one of the most versatile and distinctive of the South American aji peppers.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant after soil has warmed; chile de arbol thrives in hot conditions and produces heavily in Gulf Coast gardens throughout summer.

directions: Start indoors and transplant to warm, fertile, well-drained soil in full sun; chile de arbol (tree chili) is a tall, upright-growing Mexican pepper variety that produces prolific clusters of slender, 2–3 inch pointed red fruits that ripen from green through red and are both the primary flavor base of traditional Mexican table salsas and one of the most frequently used chilis in Mexican restaurant kitchens. The heat level is substantial (15,000–30,000 SHU) with a clean, slightly smoky, grassy flavor. They are dried and toasted whole for use in salsas de chile de arbol or ground into powder. The plant's name comes from its tree-like growth habit — stems become woody and the plant can produce prolifically for years in frost-free zones 9–11.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost and transplant to a warm, sunny location after all frost danger has passed. Hungarian wax peppers are productive and easy to grow in most climates.

directions: Start indoors and transplant to warm, fertile soil after last frost; Hungarian wax pepper produces elongated, 5–6 inch pale yellow (wax-colored) fruits that mature to red and have a medium heat level (2,500–10,000 SHU) — hotter than banana peppers but milder than jalapeños. The waxy, thick-walled fruits are outstanding for pickling (Hungarian pickled peppers are a traditional condiment), stuffing, grilling, and use in Hungarian goulash and paprikash. They are also excellent raw in salads and are often confused with banana peppers in grocery stores, though the two have different heat levels and slightly different flavors. The plants are extremely productive, producing dozens of fruits per plant through the growing season.

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; Capsicum frutescens peppers benefit from a longer indoor growing period than C. annuum. In zones 9–11 the plants grow as woody perennials and become increasingly productive in their second and third years.

directions: Start indoors early and transplant to warm, well-drained soil; piri piri is an African cultivar of Capsicum frutescens (the same species as tabasco pepper) producing small, erect, vivid red to orange fruits 1–2 inches long with an intense, bright heat (50,000–175,000 SHU). It is the essential ingredient in the famous Portuguese piri piri sauce, brought to Portugal from its colonies in Angola and Mozambique in the 1500s, and is now central to Portuguese, South African, and Mozambican cuisines. Nando's Peri-Peri sauce is based on this pepper. The fruits are blended with garlic, lemon, and oil to make a marinade-sauce for grilled chicken. Plants become perennial subshrubs in zones 9–11 and produce prolifically year-round.
sources: Purdue University Center for New Crops

planting.note: Start indoors 10–12 weeks before last frost; in zones 8–11 pequin pepper can be grown as a perennial woody shrub and wild populations persist through mild winters in south Texas. Native populations tolerate brief light freezes and resprout from roots.

directions: Start indoors and transplant to well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; pequin pepper (also spelled piquin) is a wild-type species pepper native to South Texas and northeastern Mexico, where it grows as a woody perennial shrub in dry, rocky, calcareous soils — often under the shade of mesquite, oak, and other native trees. The tiny, egg-shaped fruits (1/4 to 1/2 inch) pack an intense concentrated heat (40,000–100,000 SHU) with a distinctive citrusy, nutty flavor beloved in Tex-Mex cooking, particularly in traditional Mexican salsas and vinegar-based hot sauces. Wild-harvested pequin peppers are prized over cultivated ones in parts of northern Mexico. Birds that eat the fruits are immune to capsaicin and disperse the seeds. An excellent native plant for hot, dry, alkaline Texas and Gulf Coast gardens.
